title: AIATSIS Map
caption: Australia’s First Peoples have been living on the Australian continent for millennia. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Australia is made up of many different and distinct groups, each with their own culture, customs, language and laws. They are the world’s oldest surviving culture; cultures that continue to be expressed in dynamic and contemporary ways.
prompt: Look closely at the map and caption. What is the clearest message this source gives about Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples across Australia?
insightQuestion: Which idea is best supported by this source?
insights: There are many distinct groups across Australia, each with their own cultures, customs, languages and laws.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples formed one single culture across the whole continent.
correctInsight: There are many distinct groups across Australia, each with their own cultures, customs, languages and laws.

title: Acknowledging Country
caption: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have experienced a long history of exclusion from Australian history books, the Australian flag, the Australian anthem and for many years, Australian democracy. This history of dispossession and colonisation lies at the heart of the disparity between Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander and other Australians today. Including recognition in events, meetings and national symbols contributes to ending the exclusion that has been so damaging. Incorporating protocols recognises Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples as the Traditional Owners of land and shows respect.
prompt: This source explains why Acknowledgement of Country matters. What does it suggest these protocols are meant to do?
insightQuestion: According to the source, why are these protocols important?
insights: They help address a history of exclusion by recognising Traditional Owners and showing respect.
They exist mainly as a formal rule with no deeper purpose.
correctInsight: They help address a history of exclusion by recognising Traditional Owners and showing respect.

title: Birds, Stories and Totems
caption: Australia's native birds feature prominently in First Nations stories. These stories are vital to the sharing of First Nations knowledge, language and culture. They reflect a powerful worldview towards Country, which values and respects the relationships between all living things. Many birds are also revered and respected as totems. Aunty Rhonda Radley explains: 'A totem is something that we look after... totem is like kin, like family. And as you look after family, you would look after your totem. And that means looking after its habitat, making sure that food's available for them.'
prompt: What does this source suggest about the role of stories and totems in First Nations cultures?
insightQuestion: Which interpretation best fits Aunty Rhonda Radley’s explanation of a totem?
insights: A totem is like kin or family, and caring for it also means caring for its habitat.
A totem is mainly a decorative symbol used for art and performance.
correctInsight: A totem is like kin or family, and caring for it also means caring for its habitat.

title: Makasar Traders
caption: Macassans at Victoria, Port Essington by HS Melville. From at least 1700 until 1907, hundreds of fishermen sailed each year from Makassar on the island of Sulawesi (now Indonesia) to the northern Australian coast, an area they called ‘Marege’. The fishermen arrived each December and camped along the Arnhem Land coast, catching, boiling and drying trepang. They met, traded and worked with local Aboriginal people.
prompt: Study the image and caption. What do they suggest about contact between First Nations peoples and visitors from beyond Australia?
insightQuestion: What detail best shows that these were real working trade relationships, not brief accidental encounters?
insights: The traders met, traded and worked with local Aboriginal people over many years.
The traders avoided local people and stayed separate from them.
correctInsight: The traders met, traded and worked with local Aboriginal people over many years.

title: Trade and Transformation
caption: The months that the Makasar spent harvesting were busy ones. Fishermen speared trepang from their praus (boats) or dived to spike them with weighted harpoons. On shore, trepang were gutted and boiled in iron cauldrons, then buried in hot sand to cool. Finally, they were dried or smoked over slow fires. Each April, as the monsoon winds began to blow, the fishermen departed, returning to Makassar with holds carrying trepang to be traded north to China.
prompt: This source describes how trepang was collected, processed and moved. What does that suggest about the trade network?
insightQuestion: Which detail best shows that this trade was organised and regular?
insights: The work followed a clear seasonal pattern, with traders leaving when the monsoon winds began.
The trade happened randomly whenever people happened to find trepang.
correctInsight: The work followed a clear seasonal pattern, with traders leaving when the monsoon winds began.

title: Axe Heads Route
caption: Ground-edged axes appeared 4,500 years ago. Known as a mogo by the Gadigal, they were used as tools to make nawi (bark canoes) as well as to cut wood, hunt or build shelters. Made from strong igneous or metamorphic rock, they were traded across nations from quarry sites. Research shows that trade routes of up to 200 km were not uncommon and one axe head in the museum’s collection travelled 1000 km from its source site.
prompt: What does this source suggest about the scale and sophistication of First Nations trade networks?
insightQuestion: Which conclusion is best supported by the evidence in this source?
insights: Some tools travelled extremely long distances, showing broad and organised trade networks.
Trade was very local and rarely moved goods beyond one nearby group.
correctInsight: Some tools travelled extremely long distances, showing broad and organised trade networks.

title: Symbolism of the Flag
caption: The Torres Strait Islander flag design was adopted in 1992. The green stripes represent the land, the black stripes represent the people, and the blue represents the sea. In July 1995, the Government decided the flag should be proclaimed a 'Flag of Australia' under section 5 of the Flags Act 1953. It was so proclaimed by the Governor General on 14 July 1995.
prompt: What does the symbolism in this source suggest about Torres Strait Islander identity?
insightQuestion: Which interpretation best fits the details in the source?
insights: The flag expresses identity through links to land, people and sea.
The flag is mainly important because it copies other national flag designs.
correctInsight: The flag expresses identity through links to land, people and sea.

title: Crocodile Mask
caption: Masks made of turtle shell were used in rituals and ceremonies in the Torres Strait Islands and made to represent ancestors with an associated totem, frequently the crocodile. The masks sometimes have the form of the human face or animals such as birds, fish or reptiles. Similar masks and headdresses made of plywood, bamboo or plastic continue to be created and used today.
prompt: What does this artefact suggest about the importance of ceremony, ancestors and symbolism in Torres Strait Islander culture?
insightQuestion: What was the main purpose of these masks according to the source?
insights: They were used in rituals and ceremonies to represent ancestors and associated totems.
They were made mainly as practical tools for fishing and hunting.
correctInsight: They were used in rituals and ceremonies to represent ancestors and associated totems.

title: Traditional Canoe
caption: Dauarti canoe belonging to Bamaga Family, Saibai, Torres Strait Island, June 1931. This source shows a watercraft from the Torres Strait, where travel by sea was an important part of life, movement and connection between islands.
prompt: What does this source suggest about the role of the sea in Torres Strait Islander life?
insightQuestion: Which conclusion is best supported by this canoe source?
insights: Travel, movement and connection across the sea were important parts of Torres Strait Islander life.
The sea had little influence on Torres Strait Islander daily life and movement.
correctInsight: Travel, movement and connection across the sea were important parts of Torres Strait Islander life.
